points.text: Analyze and reduce the duration of DB transactions causing lock contention (e.g., shorten critical sections).
Add or tune indexes on login‑related tables to speed up SELECT/UPDATE queries and lower lock time.
Implement connection pooling to limit concurrent DB connections and avoid overload spikes.
Introduce rate limiting or exponential back‑off on login endpoints to mitigate brute‑force attempts.
Deploy CAPTCHA or multi‑factor authentication for repeated failed logins from the same IP range.
Monitor DB lock metrics and login failure rates after each change to validate stability and security improvements.
Add indexes on login query columns (e.g., username, email) to cut row scans and shorten lock duration.
Switch authentication queries to READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT or lower isolation to avoid shared locks.
Offload authentication reads to a replica or cache (e.g., Redis) to reduce primary DB contention.
Implement rate limiting / IP throttling for failed logins to curb brute‑force traffic.
Enable account lockout after a configurable number of failed attempts to improve security.
Add rate limiting (e.g., iptables, fail2ban, or web server) on login attempts per IP to reduce brute‑force load.
Enable TCP SYN cookies and increase backlog queue (net.core.somaxconn) to handle burst of connections.
Deploy a load balancer or reverse proxy to spread login traffic across multiple app servers, reducing DB contention.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: Investigate DB lock contention (e.g., query optimization, indexing, or adding read replicas).
Use Linux kernel tuning (e.g., net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse, net.core.somaxconn) to improve socket handling under high load.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The question concerns database performance and security, which is outside the math_logic domain.
UNKNOWN: No applicable algebraic or logical reasoning can be provided for this query.
Add rate limiting/throttling on login attempts per IP or user to curb brute‑force traffic and reduce DB load.
Introduce a CAPTCHA or challenge after a few failed attempts to block automated attacks.
Implement temporary account lockout after repeated failures to protect accounts and lower DB contention.
Offload credential verification to a read‑replica or cache (e.g., Redis) to reduce write‑lock contention on the primary DB.
